did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep convolutional neural network based on the Inception.v.3 architecture with transfer learning to classify skin diseases, specifically melanoma and seborrhoeic keratosis, achieving 91% accuracy and an AUC-ROC of 0.96.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a deep convolutional neural network (Inception.v.3) for skin disease recognition. It details specific methods (transfer learning, data augmentation), reports original results (91% accuracy, AUC-ROC 0.96), and describes the implementation of a web service, which constitutes new technical results rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: Artificial intelligence image recognition inhealthcare
journal: 2018 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Applications and Innovations (IC-AIAI)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma and skin disease recognition by developing a deep convolutional neural network based on the Inception.v.3 architecture with transfer learning. The model was trained on 10,000 skin lesion images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, augmented to 1,000,000 samples using state-of-the-art techniques. Evaluation on an independent test set of 1,000 images demonstrated a classification accuracy of 91% and an AUC-ROC of 0.96. Additionally, the system achieved 97% accuracy in distinguishing seborrhoeic keratosis from melanoma and nevi. The authors implemented a free web service for telemedicine-based self-diagnosis and concluded that the method is comparable to clinical dermatoscopy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary malignant tumor studied; model achieved 91% accuracy for skin disease recognition and 97% accuracy in distinguishing melanoma from nevi/seborrhoeic keratosis.
Benign lesion studied; model achieved 97% accuracy in recognizing seborrhoeic keratosis.
Benign lesion used as a comparator class to distinguish from melanoma.
General category of images from the ISIC archive used for training (10,000 images) and testing (1,000 images) the classification model.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease categories (melanoma, seborrhoeic keratosis, nevus, and general skin diseases)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a deep convolutional neural network (Inception.v.3) used for 'skin diseases recognition' and 'classification'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories, specifically distinguishing melanoma from seborrhoeic keratosis and nevi. The study reports classification accuracy (91% for skin disease recognition, 97% for seborrhoeic keratosis) and AUC-ROC (0.96), which are metrics for classification tasks. The output is used for binary/multiclass diagnosis (melanoma vs. benign lesions) rather than predicting a continuous numeric value.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive
datasets_used.description: A public archive of skin lesions images obtained during real clinical researches. The study used a subset of 10,000 skin disease images from this archive for training the model.

use.training: Used as the primary training dataset. The 10,000 images were augmented to 1,000,000 samples using rotation, mirroring, noise addition, and luminance changes.
use.other: The archive was also referenced as a source of data that may contain interferences/noise.

datasets_used.name: Independent Test Set
datasets_used.description: An independent subsample of images used for evaluation, not used for training.

use.testing: Used as a control test sequence to evaluate the model's performance, achieving 91% accuracy and 0.96 AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet Challenge Data Archive
datasets_used.description: A large-scale visual recognition dataset used for pretraining the reference Inception.v.3 model.

use.training: Used for pretraining the Inception.v.3 architecture before transfer learning was applied to the skin disease task.

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance of the deep convolutional neural network on an independent test set of 1,000 images.
To measure the proportion of correct predictions made by the model on the independent test set (91% for skin disease recognition) and specific lesion types (97% for seborrhoeic keratosis).
To improve classification results by combining predictions from an ensemble of 5 networks with similar structure but different weights.
To expand the training dataset from 10,000 to 1,000,000 samples to mitigate the limited training set size and improve model generalization.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy (91% for skin disease recognition, 97% for seborrhoeic keratosis) and AUC-ROC (0.96) on an independent test set.

ISIC Archive (Training).notes: The training set consisted of 10,000 skin disease images from the ISIC archive, which were augmented using rotation, mirroring, noise addition, and luminance changes to create 1,000,000 samples. The model was pretrained on ImageNet Challenge data.

Independent Test Set.notes: The model was evaluated on an independent test set of 1,000 images. It achieved 91% accuracy for general skin disease recognition and an AUC-ROC of 0.96. Specifically, the model could recognize seborrhoeic keratosis with 97% accuracy and distinguish melanoma from nevus. The abstract also mentions accuracy of 94% for dermoscopic pictures and 88% for microscopic ones, though the main results section specifies 91% overall accuracy on the test set.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet Challenge data archive (ILSVRC) before transfer learning was applied to skin diseases classification.

architecture_details.modification: Transfer learning applied by deleting outer layers and adding new neurons for skin diseases classification.
architecture_details.ensemble: Ensemble of 5 networks with similar structure but different weights.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep convolutional neural network based on Inception.v.3 architecture
Ensemble of 5 Inception.v.3 networks with majority voting
IBM Watson system (mentioned as related work/comparison)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotation, mirroring, noise addition, parting, and luminance conditions changing (expanded 10,000 training images to 1,000,000 samples)
Input resizing: Reference model (Inception.v.3) used 300x300 pixel images; final model applied convolutional layers to use higher image scales with 700x700 pixel input size

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of state-of-the-art augmentation methods to expand the training set from 10,000 to 1,000,000 samples. Specific angles or probabilities not reported.
Applied as part of state-of-the-art augmentation methods to expand the training set from 10,000 to 1,000,000 samples. Specific probabilities not reported.

conclusions: The proposed deep convolutional neural network based on the Inception.v.3 architecture with transfer learning achieved a skin disease recognition accuracy of 91% and an AUC-ROC of 0.96 on an independent test set of 1,000 images.
The model demonstrated high performance in distinguishing specific lesion types, achieving 97% accuracy in recognizing seborrhoeic keratosis and effectively differentiating melanoma from nevi.
The method is comparable to classic clinical dermatoscopy methods used by specialists for skin disease diagnosis.
The system is capable of operating effectively with a limited training set (10,000 images) by utilizing state-of-the-art data augmentation techniques to expand the dataset to 1,000,000 samples.

The authors implemented a free web service (skincheckup.online) for telemedicine-based self-diagnosis of melanoma risk, indicating the method's potential for integration into telemedicine systems for complex human health analysis.
important_remarks: The training dataset was small (10,000 images from the ISIC archive) and potentially noisy or containing interferences, necessitating extensive data augmentation (expanding to 1,000,000 samples) and transfer learning.
The study reports conflicting accuracy figures: 91% overall accuracy in the main results section, but 94% for dermoscopic pictures and 88% for microscopic pictures in the abstract.
The test set consisted of only 1,000 independent images, which is a relatively small sample size for validating clinical performance.
The paper does not report sensitivity or specificity values for the overall classification task, only mentioning 'sensitivity of the best of our models' without providing the specific number.
The model was evaluated on an independent test set, but there is no mention of external validation on a completely separate, geographically distinct dataset.

The web service implementation is described as being in 'testing mode' with ongoing communication with medical centers, indicating it was not fully deployed or clinically validated at the time of publication.
The study focuses on binary/multiclass classification (melanoma vs. benign) rather than complex differential diagnosis or segmentation, limiting its scope compared to comprehensive clinical systems.
